speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In this conversation with Floyd Lawrence and Nola Wilkins Lawrence took place at the home of Mr. and Mrs. Carl Lancaster at Helmer on January 21, 1976. Because other people were visiting in the home at the time of the interview, it is difficult to understand in the first few minutes before we move to an adjacent room.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And your elbow will freeze. He lied to you. He said it was only going to take a little while. He was just teasing. I know. He was teasing. And then we got over here to where the old swimming building was where his folks lay in the air. Or in the down.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And I said how much farther?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: It's about halfway. And I thought, oh, we'll never get to the pool. And then bushes and slappas in your face, you know. We may have that old mountain.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did the kids ride? Did you ride in the back or ride in the front?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, we rode up in the seat with him. Ride along with him. I see. You've got quite a lot of hauling of people around Troy. There he is. There it is. There it is. Oh, yeah. But that doctor up there, he'd come in and you'd want to raise my eyebrows. Now this picture. That's what made her so mad. Come on, you'll raise your eyes. And that'd make me so mad. Because I couldn't do it. I knew she couldn't. I knew she couldn't. Here's a chair by a guy. Oh, it ain't something. Oh, here, let me. Yeah. I'll tell you a thing about you. He was a stupid doctor and then she'd cry. She'd say that she had a stupid doctor she was in. I said he couldn't be too stupid or he wouldn't be up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the store like then? What did they sell? What were people buying?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They bought a church. Well, he brought some more for you. Something very good for you. That's the darndest pantry don't lay down. He walks across the floor. It's a stained black and the large stuff. Well, Jerome Ryan's got the darndest dog now. He's just a pup. Then he'll sit there and he'll get so sleepy. He'll nod. He'll get sleepy and nod. He won't lay down like a kid. He will not lay down. You figured that all out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was that at?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: It was down on Bear Creek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yeah, you mean the one with the bird thing over there?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Just all morning long. Yeah, on Bear Creek. I don't know if you can get over my line. See, you took him past Mrs. Emo Nelson. She went right out on her own. Right down there to Bear Creek. I don't know. Pay attention to her. Then she'll run over to me and nudge me with her nose and look back at Arnold. She didn't do something about it. This was at the store. I think she wants me to see that Arnold's going to want you to meet her. I don't know what Arnold told you about it. I think that I can make Arnold do it. But she didn't look like she didn't do something about it. This one was rebuilt here. They brought that one in and rebuilt. If I'm in the bedroom and I'm in the south and you get your apartment and I want him while I send him and her out and then I get her and then I get her and then I want him. That's the one that Arnold told about. She fell in the market.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Mary and honey mewell stayed away?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Arnold was there. He hadn't gone to work in the house. She got me going. I remember them being around the wells. I started to get up and I started to walk sideways. Most of them were really good. She was a wonderful person.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And he was but he was rough. I'm sure it was quick for you to learn those but for sure. And after you got her bed. Yeah they stick. Yeah right. Yeah. The boys. I was talking to a few of the Settle family. They were, they were black. We knew that. They lived in your monster world. The one, the one you settled. We still live in. I think you're the best thing you've ever taken. I'm going to take your hand. That's for my mom. I believe it's a little.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: The only one that was here. That family. But you know the people mixed with them the same as they did white people.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well they, they really were more like white people. You know cause they were among, they were the only family among the white people at that time. Colored people. They were colored and the rest were white. Anyway they went to those kids with the white kids to school and naturally they kind of become more white, acting more white than, and Mary stayed with me.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I'm going to have to leave. Of course she went to Spokane.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: She was our neighbor. In later years Mary came with us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did she go to Spokane? When did she leave the area here? Was it after, was it before the second world war?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh very after the war. Yeah it was afterwards. Of course she built this, she had this building brought down and rebuilt down here. Gene Settle was telling me about that the guy that she had been married to from Spokane disappeared during World War I. No that was her, her king. That was her, the father to the, to the, to the Eastern and Bell. Right. Yeah. And he did, he disappeared and she never knew what the king was. Well he was an army officer.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He was a pretty well educated nigger. Yes he was. And he was, he was one of the, in the middle suits and stuff, in the trade.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He was a tailor. Yeah. He was a tailor, her husband. He had a tailor shop in the middle. But they didn't get along too well after his children was born and he just disappeared and nobody just left him. Well that must have made it pretty hard on her. Well it did but you know she had been used to, she was like her mother. She's used to working and she, she did housework for people and, and she washed, took in washing and, and so she made a living for the children. And of course Roy, her brother would stay there with her and I suppose he paid her board too you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Chuck, didn't he work in this?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah. Yeah, that's what I, I've heard, you know some people have said that, that Joe was really strong, a very strong man.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Other people have said that it was Chuck that was really strong. No it was Joe. It was Joe. Joe was a pretty good man. You know he was a lot better than any man in this country. You'd get in the rig and go to town with him, everybody would buy a just wave you know. Oh they were well liked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did Joe get this reputation for being, being strong? As an auditor, a fighter, what did he do?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, he, he held up for his rice yolk. A lot more than Chuck but, they wasn't either one. Outstanding, that way I don't doubt. Don't seem like it to me. I'll let tell you what happened on time. There's a bunch of natural meadows up above here. And I and him was up there mowing hay. And I had a new mower and he had an old one. His keep breaking down a little. And it was real hot. And I was helping get his mower fixed. He was, I was ahead of him and I made it around to come up to him and then I stopped. And that wild grass was real hard to cut. You had to have an awful good mower to cut it. And so I was laying out on the grass there pretty close to him helping him.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He says to me, I said to him, Joe what makes you smell like that when you get hot?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well he said you smell that way to me too. Well I said I'll get away from you then.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: But he, he was quite an old character now I think. But he was a strong, to be a colored person. And him the only family in the country. He was a strong person that way. He held up for his rights and he was a hard worker. And he worked in the woods and he hauled prey. And he was just a strong character that way. Physically.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Lou?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I've heard that, when Jean Settle was telling me that Lou didn't care very much for some of the harder drinking ways. No she was a very moderate woman that way. And he was pretty bad to drink. No she didn't go in for that and she had a hard life that way. Cause he drank quite heavy. And she was such a good kind soul.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Jean said she really worked at it and just took it in silence. Did her work. Always kept food on the table. Quite a fun. Always. And she did. She was a good cook.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: You know they still remember in Troy. The old timers still tell about how Joey's coming to Troy to go drink in there. There were a couple of times there were stories about him standing up to, I don't know if you ever heard of Marshall Hayes. Hayes was this guy that got shot in Troy about nineteen four. He lived just below where he was shot. Just below the water table.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh really?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: At that time. That must have been when?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In nineteen four. Nineteen four was the year he was shot. That's the way I understand it.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I know he was often scared all of us. You know we as little kids and that was going on. We could see right up there but of course we didn't. I was too small to realize. You were there when it happened. Oh yes. We lived right down there when it happened. And now let me see.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He was shot by who? Fry?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Jake Sly. Right. Yeah he was shot. It was Pang Sly that did the shooting I thought.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Was it?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I thought it was Pang Sly.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yes.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Jake Sly lived by us. That's right. Jake Sly lived by us. And right next door. I think he was a brother if I remember right. They opened the door and let him in. Oh I tell you the people in Troy was really mad and they got their guns out and they were just about ready to go clean. Kill him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sly you mean?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah they sly. But then they arrested him and that was it. But then on the other hand I heard that Hays was not at all well liked and a lot of people called him a son. Well I guess he really wasn't. I don't know. Hi. Hi. And I don't know. Hi there.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: How are you? How are you?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: It's good to have seen you guys. Glad to see you too.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Hi.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: How are you? How are you? Oh how are ya?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Fine. And he looked the situation over. Yeah he was up here and looked it all over. They had the store. The store was here then. And they thought it would be a good business because they was putting the railroad through you see. And of course those men they kind of liked to get out you know after they got their checks. So they would rent the horses or hire him to take them to Troy or Mobile or wherever they wanted to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that his main, the main people that used the delivery bar was the lumberjacks and the people building the railroad?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah and the railroad. The men that was working on the railroad. And you know he'd count us up here a lot before I was ever here. Well at that time there was trees all around here. Oh yes there was trees up here. See here where they cut these stumps. Oh yeah. And then that was the Granny's house. It wasn't a very big house but it was a...

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Well now what was this?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: That's a comfort house. I can't figure who built that. I just turn around here and listen to her. This is the way it sat. And this is a, we went to school to her with Annamarie. Yes I remember her mentioning you and I talked to her. She sure remembers you folks. Oh yes.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Who's that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Annamarie. Well she talked before when she was 18 or a little before she was 18. Well see I went to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she teach you two terms?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were going to school?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: She taught me too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah. She was at one of her first classes. I mean she was very young then still. Yes she wasn't quite 18. She couldn't collect her wages. Her dad couldn't collect them. He took them too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about that when she was teaching school?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: That was her first term.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Then she came back a few years later and she taught another what two terms?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh no. You see Oslun was her name. Yeah Oslun. Well that was after she was married. Oh she was married. That was her maiden name. What was the...

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: who taught with her?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Harriet Fairbanks. The second time she taught. Well that's when I went to school to hear.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the school term when she was here teaching you?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh I'm not thinking a year so much as... Oh yes I should say. I tell you she was an outdoor person. She'd get on her skis and go over these fences, take a bunch of the boys you know and go over these fences. This old bachelor down here he said that she broke his fences down. He got kind of mad at her you know. She always took the boys out. She always took the boys out. Oh yes. And she was a... She drove to the mountain. And this was our first train.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: First train?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: The first we all went over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was the one that came from Potlatch with all the brass and all came up to Bow Hill? Is that when they were coming all the way to Bow Hill?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah. Well I don't know whether they were there that day or not. Well I don't know if they were but... It was the first excursion train. That was the main railroad track to run the Bow Hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Everybody went out to look at that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh yeah. That was something. That was a new railroad through here.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: You know that was something to have a railroad coming up through here because they'd been traveling with just horses and wagons. And the roads were terrible. Yes. Well yes it was scenario you know that the limbs had hit you you know and when you're riding along and it was that kind of road. Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the train stop in Helmer? Did they have any ceremonies or anything?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No. No. Just the people went over there to see it go through. That was just such an occasion you know.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: My mother's in there and I think Floyd's mother but it's a faint you can't see it of course. I don't know if you have it on here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who are you going to give it to?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I don't think they put it away at once.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had your father been a...had he run a livery barn before?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Was this his first... No. No.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He came to Troy in 1900 and he had a dray. He bought out the dray business there and he ran two teams. And then he had went into partnership with Ben Nelson in a feed store and then he had a little confectionary store later on. And he contracted a typhoid fever and he had to give his store up. And of course then he lost his foot. It settled in his foot and he had to have it amputated. And then we...the next deal was up here then. The lid was stable.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He had one eye. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't he get that cut in the corn patch?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah. He fell and he could see but the lid was gone down. And then there were days they never...they didn't do anything about it you know when it could have been fixed. Because he could see out of it but he couldn't lift his lid you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that happen to him here or was it back before he came? Did that happen in Idaho here?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, it was back in Kentucky before he came here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the family come straight to here from Kentucky?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: To Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they know people in Troy?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well his father was there. His father had come ahead of him you see in his family. And so then they just thought he'd make a better living out here and so they moved to Troy. That was in 1900. And my granddad Wilkins was already here at that time. From his family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well is...are you related to Kenneth Wilkins?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, cousin.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Cousin to Kenneth.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And then my mother's folks came later on, the Leagues. They came out of that. And they lived in Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your parents meet in Troy then? Pardon?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Did your parents meet in Troy or had they gotten married back in... Oh no, they were all married when they came out. Well there was four of us kids in our family, in the Wilkins family. Edgie Wilkins family.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well now you better tell them something about your family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well yeah, your family was...weren't they about the first family? Was it at McGarry Meadows?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They were the first family. My granddad, I don't think he ever proved up on McGarry Meadows, but he squatted on that and built a house on it. And I think he sold it. And he left here and went to Nesbursk and they opened up that reservation. He was a funny guy. He would go in the place and pick it up just so he could live good. Somebody would come along and buy it and he'd go somewhere else. Start right over again. I never figured that out. You know, I've read anyway that a lot of guys did that in the early...you know, the real first...the early pioneers did that a lot. They'd move on.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well he was a carpenter too, wasn't he?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, he was a natural homeowner. He was a good carpenter. Well maybe he liked to build houses. Well I think he did. Like to build himself a new one.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well the last one he built, he built down on the...was it the Sweetwater?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah. And part of it's still staying.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On the Sweetwater in Wyoming?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No. No, down here by Lapway. Oh, oh yeah. Up that way.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well so, he was out here but did...were his kids here at the same time with him? His...your grandfather...your father? Was he here too?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They moved from Iowa, didn't they?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah. Not straight to Idaho. No, they didn't move. They moved to Dayton.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, down around Dayton.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And then they stayed there a while and then they came up here.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: In what was it, 1900?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was your grandfather gone by then?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was still here when your father came?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, he come...the whole family come together. Yeah. There was a...I don't know, Dad had about four brothers and two sisters and they all come. And down here were...this guy bought my Dad's place.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They stayed there the...well, my Dad stayed there the first winter and then my granddad, he went on up and Gary met us. And he built a house there, a pretty nice house. And then he sold it. Told his rights to Gary.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder, when he come to McGarry Meadows, there must have hardly been anybody there yet, was there?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I mean, it must have been pretty empty around there. Well, there wasn't no other families there. And I think there was a family that lived at the Forks, a potlatch up there.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: What were their names?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Carlson's.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Carlson's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you figure why that people hadn't moved up in here before? I mean, it was when 1900 there was still so empty?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: It was all covered with timber and there wasn't much of a way to make a living. Now, my Dad said when he come here, you could drive a team of horses anywhere you wanted to go around this country. It was all clean.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, like Burke said, the underbrush was gone. The maniocs kept it burned off.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And they kept it burned close enough so that it didn't make a hot enough fire to kill the winners. I got a little soul and a little pitch to do and I want to fix it all up so the soul was all burned. And I know my Dad said if he could have burned it, he'd have burned everything and he could have burned it all if he could have burned it all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did your father know about it? Where did he start? Did he build himself a place too?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, the whole family lived there one winter and they built this house and then they went on, see, and my Dad stayed there.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I said to my Dad one time, how come you didn't go at the reservation?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: You know, that's good soil over there. Well, he said, son, I was just a poor, I couldn't go, that was all.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, how old was he, I wonder about then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he grown?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, yeah. He was 21 when he got here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, what did he do to make a living after the rest of them went?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He went out in the spruce country and worked in the harvest field. He was a sacksover and he done that for years. Us kids and mothers stayed there at home for ourselves. But that's the way they got by the winters.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He can make enough money selling sacks to support the family for the rest of the year?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: That must not have taken too much. Well, it's a lot different now.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, they accumulated a little stock, you know, too, to help the land. They had some cows. They just gradually built up, you know, just like any homesteader.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he homesteading then or did he get land for somebody?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, he homesteaded. It was 1890, Arthur Brooke tells us, and I think our papers are home. I have all this at home. 1890 when the launches came. See, I got this from Arthur Brooke, partly for this book. Oh. You see. And I just scribbled it down as he told us about it. Uh -huh. And I helped Miller go around and get some of it. Yes, I realize that because, well, both your names are in here quite a bit, but he thanks you, especially at the beginning of the book here. There's one or two mistakes in there, but in my book now I corrected them.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: But... It's inevitable. You have to, you've got to make mistakes because, you know, you don't have a chance to check with everybody.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I thought he did a real good job at this part myself. He did. He did. He put in the whole... And he tried to get the real facts as near as he could, you know.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And he was very careful. He was at our house there for two weeks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you living up here then or were you down there?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, we lived here.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: We've been down there three years.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, there's a house that my mother lived, another log house. I seen it just... You get it right back there just a little bit.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh no, what's that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Mother's log house, where mother lived.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, you mean... I don't know how much you can get your old name on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's this way?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: The name on it. He said I just...

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I just seen it. I see it the other way going.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Now this was an old family. This was a family that lived up there on Gary Butte.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the name?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: That was Hampton Fell. And see the store, that's the only store that was here. It was a Janesville store and his folks ran it. These homesteaders would have to walk from those homesteads clear down here to get groceries. Some of them didn't have horses either.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And it would take them all day to walk down from up there on the McGarry Butte down to the store. And to his store. You know, I never forget his wife. This old Hampton Fell was, I don't know, he wasn't very well. He was kind of a fat guy. His wife was a little old skinny woman and she come down to my dad and she wanted some groceries. He said no, he said I can't sell you anymore. He said you've got too many now. You know that woman just stood there and cried. That was hard for anybody to tell him that to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did your father say then?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, he finally let her have my food pile.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: It was bad for him. Yeah, rough. You can see that cabin here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When your family there, when you were a kid at McGarry, when the man was around, well actually where was his homestead? Was it right at Janesville?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Right where he later built the store?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, that's where the store was. See he had the post office and they'd bring the mail from Troy through by team.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And that was the center post office. They would get their mail from all these homesteaders did from there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that was before there was anything in Helmer?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh yeah. Well when he started, he started with a homestead, he didn't have the store right away. No, no. Not when he had to go out and work to support the family. It just kept building up. Now this is your picture there. This is our picture we have taken from the other view. That's quite a bunch. This is all of the family, all of the people, most of them. They're all gone now.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well Marvin Miller is in here. He's in his picture. Yeah. May Wilkins is in here too.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Now this is McGarry. He was the father of Jim that owned McGarry Meadows. And this was my brother. Now they made a mistake, this was Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence, but that was my brother, Lloyd Wilkins there. And this is our school here. That's Anna Marie. Oh, wow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Those were all the kids that were in the school then?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Not too many. Well that day, I think there was some on the 6th or something. There was just a few. Well there was quite a school here at one time. There was around 50 enrolled here. Well there was two teachers too. Yeah.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: But this means a lot to us because we know every one of those people. And here are my sisters little old runt here. I don't know where she's at. That's her in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When your father had the store, in those early years there, how much of the community was there around there? Was there many other families?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well there was quite a few homesteaders around. Not too far away. They come in and homestead that to get that stimulus. If you can read this, this is them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The list that they put over there?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: That's the list that he gave us. And he has, but I just scribbled that down because we were in such a hurry. But that was part of the homesteaders. But McGarry wasn't on there, but they come later on. McGarry's and Hollenbeck's and the Woodges were up there on McGarry Mountain. Woods.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I'd like to read that too. I've interviewed her quite a few times. I spent a lot of time talking with her. The early days. We've got very good memories here. She knows because she lived up there in those days. It was tough.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: That's the idea that I get.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it tough because there was no way to make a living?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well yes, and they were trying to hold this land down. They figured they could sell it later on.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: That was the idea, I guess, wasn't it, for these homesteads?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They got this timber, but a lot of them didn't get anything out of that timber. That company would buy it for nothing if they did get it. They didn't make no big money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they holding it to sell to the company? Or were they hoping that it would turn into farming later? What were they thinking?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They opened up a little ground. They all had a garden. That kind of stuff. That helped them a lot. In the back of their mind, they figured they'd sell this timber sometimes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The company got an awful cheat, didn't they?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: When they bought around, I understand it was something like three dollars a thousand. They didn't pay that much for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, if you cut it and hauled it yourself?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, my dad doesn't love that.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He bought it and then they'd loan it to you. He never made no big money out of it. They didn't pay that much over there. They were there for stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there quite a few homesteaders there before potlatch ever came in? Before they ever came in with the railroad?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, they homesteaded along hot before they ever came in.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They didn't come in here with the railroad until about 1970. About 1970.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And you know, up around the campaign there, that was the nicest stand in my life. It finally was in the world.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: It wasn't too big, but it stood thick. It was just as straight as could be. People from Moscow would drive clear up here at the camp in the southern part. And then the team met family. And there was always someone there. But that was sure a wonderful stand in the White Pine. At that time, they had more White Pine than there was anywhere in the United States.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: But you know, they sent down all over the world that White Pine. It was light lumber, light grain, and it was awful soft. It was awful nice to work. That's the reason it was so. I know when I built my covers down here, it cost me $150 a thousand for the White Pine. They got big money for that.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I suppose they were trying to make a lot of money to pay off their new solvent. They wanted to make a lot of money right at the beginning. Well, no, it was a big enough company that they had money enough to operate on. They cut a White Pine down at their camp. They took down that and it stood for years right out in the right of the depot there. And I don't remember, it was eight feet across the town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the one he calls the Giant White Pine in there? Or do you think that's a different one?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I wonder. Well, there's one standing up above Harvard there. It's a big White Pine. I don't think it's quite as big as this one was up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they just cut it down because it was for the lumber in it?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, they cut the lumber out of it, all of it. They saved this twelve foot piece and set it up there for the whole movie. I imagine they started that too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever warn the Warren family at all? No, the Warrens that were there before Bovill?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I was a little too young to know much about them. I heard my folks talking about them. They knew them well.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: That's where Bovill stands now, is the Warren Meadows.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I heard my dad say that he had a bunch of cattle and they run out on that matter. He said them was the fattest thing he ever saw. Like I knew some kids. He said they were sure pretty cattle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many people run cattle on them meadows in the summer?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Summertime, yeah. Summertime they did. But the winters were too long. They couldn't make them long because it took too much. They had to feed them six months out of the year. They had to feed them a little bit.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I know when I was a kid my dad had 50 to 100 heads. That was my job to look at in the summertime. To keep track of them and know where they were at.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to move them around or did they stay on one meadow?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, they just rolled around. They stayed in the fall when they got dry. They'd go for the thick timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you going to say?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I was going to tell you, my dad bought a Roam Durham bull. He was going to tell you a story now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you want to hear that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, it sounds good already. Now you be careful what you say. Don't make it too strong. Well, he sent my brother out after the day before. My brother found it but he couldn't get it home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So my dad came down to where I was at and said, You've gone past that bull tomorrow?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I said, yeah. So I had an old dog that was just eating that thing up on his feet. I went up there where it was at. There was a family named Erickson and they had some cattle in this bull. I just sent the dog over and he ran around there a little bit and pretty soon he took off for home. So I followed along the line and we got down to Chez Mettez. Well, there was a little shack there and there was a little bit of a mooch. He didn't stand over that high.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: So this bull could run just about as fast as that horse could. I was running for about a quarter of a mile and just nipping and tapping. Finally, the bull jumped over the fence and went down to the house. So I set up there on my horse for a while and I didn't see no bull or nothing. The dog got off the horse and walked down to the house. The toilet sat right up on the kind of little bank right outside the house. I heard the darkness rustling in that toilet. I looked around and here comes this little woman now and she couldn't talk. She was just catching her breath and going on.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Finally, she came down to where I was at and I said, What's the matter with you?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I thought she was going crazy or something. I grabbed a hold of her and just shook her and she said, The bull, the bull.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I said, Where's he at?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: She said, He's in the house. So I went in there and she was in the living room and she went through the kitchen and she had every door behind her and went out and set the door on the toilet.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: So I said to her, How did he get in here?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: She said, I was just straightening up the diving port and I looked around and that bull's head was right in the door. So she left then and this old dog, he sat there. He didn't go in the house. He just sat in the door. The bull was back by the piano when I got there. I made the dog get out of the road and I took the whip and I hit the noise. I said, Well, we'll have the bull whip and I gave him a lick of that and he went out on that floor to go down that far every time he stepped. So I went down to the grain field then and I went down to the grain field and I went back up the house and I was helping her get up there right off the road. I was helping her get when they were running.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: So she got a drag on laughing then. She kept laughing and I was helping her roll up the road and get outdoors. She just kept right on laughing.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I said, What are you laughing about?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I don't see nothing very funny about that. And you know, every time she'd see me after that, she'd start right in laughing. There it is.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: This was her first. That was the Parkes' home at Avon. That's Floyd's grandmother.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Grandma and grandfather.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that any relation to Mrs. Grannis' family?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No. I've seen that cabin before that picture. They've tore it down now, but it's if you stand down here by the highway going to Harvard down there at Avon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's where you were born?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, this is a little... Once you were born, she come from Kansas. But that was her hometown. She came back one day. She picked up on them. That's where they lived. So she met your father right out here. She met your father right here in this country. Yeah.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: My dad was quite a musician. In these early days, he made his living by playing the violin mostly. Playing for dance. You know, us kids all had got a little of that in us. We used to put in... Every month we'd send after new music. We'd get that music just about learned from some old kids. We'd set up lots of nights at two or three o 'clock in the morning practicing that music. We got pretty good too. That was in your later years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you just play different instruments? What did you play?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Trombone. I played lots of times after I got a big enough. I played long enough that I'd look right down and see my lips. I swear to God.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These dances that your father would play at in the early days here, where would they be? Different people's houses?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: There you go. Schoolhouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it just be him by himself or would there be other people playing it too?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, he most generally had a second with him. When I learned to play the banjo, I wasn't big enough to reach up and tune it. I had to take it down and put it on my lap to get it in tune. But, you know, he used to practice with me. He could play all night and never play the same tune.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: So he used to tell me, he used to teach me to go on time and he'd get anything else.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And you know, one night we went to Pullman's television broadcast, a fitness contest, and I said to him, Well, Dad, what are you going to play?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I said, Now listen, you ain't played for a long time. You better be getting to figure out something. So they had stalls in there. I don't know what kind of room it was, but I suppose that was about 12 by 10 feet. You just went in there, it was all open on one side. We went in there and he started scratching around around that. And I said to him, Well, you've got to be better than that. So he said, I said, I'm going to open up on this old grandiose. I said, You better bear down a little, Harvey. So he did. And you know, it wasn't 10 minutes, so I bet there was 100 violins in there to listen for. And there was a room in there that was hard here. And I put in quite a time with her. She breaks the bed. And you know, before I left there, I picked blisters on my fingers and picked them off. Well, that was in later years after Mary.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know the kind of music he played? What kind of stuff did he like to play around at schoolhouses?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, that time it was... Well, not then. He means in the earlier days, Dad. The earlier days, yeah. When he played at the schoolhouses and the clubs. Well, it was mostly cadreels and... Squared ancestors. Squared, and waltzes and two -stairs.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He knew that music without no sheet music or anything. No, he couldn't reason to know it. But he had some brothers that really went... He had one that played in Spora Wola on radio. And he guaranteed to play any requests anybody asked him. And I guess he continued to do that too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did your family come from? Before they come here?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, they were born in England. And they come to... Iowa. Iowa. Kansas. And they moved from Iowa to... Around Dayton. Yeah. Around Dayton country. That's when they first come from the East. I wonder where he picked up the music himself. If he learned it in England or if he learned it in... No. They were just natural. They were just natural. The family was naturally musical. You know, I played after I got bigger. And I could hear a tune and sit right down and play it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You still play at all?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No. No, I did play after I moved down there. Until he had a stroke. No. You played until you had your stroke. And then they come to me and they wanted me to play with them. There was a bunch of them. And then the kids was in the army and we was going to play to give them some money when they come home. We started in and we played enough to pay for the Grange Hall. We played for a band, or piano, about that. And then we could play every night if we wanted. We wouldn't do it without work and I couldn't do it without it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which Grange Hall was this?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Derrick. Derrick.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, you didn't pay for all the Grange Hall. You helped with your music and you donate your time. Yeah. And then they donated their time for these dances so that the money, the proceeds, would be divided among the service members. And they'd come in when they'd come home so they'd have a little night to spend, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you play around with your father in these dances in their earlier days?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, I wasn't old enough for the guy. But he was a good boy.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Who played with him in the earlier days?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: There was three or four played together. They had what they called a bowery. They used to call them a bowery. Now we would call them a, what would we call it, a pavilion. They made it out of poles, open air, you know, for the Fourth of July.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would they do that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And that was down here on the hill above where they lived, Grangesville. Well, up where the Lawrence House is now. They just put that up for the summer vacation. They used to have dances there in this open air. Just put up a pole, you know, but it was open. And they put a lot of dances there. And then he played at the schoolhouse and he played over here in a building later on. And then there was other older, older schoolhouses, you know, that would grow and play.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you talking about... or his dad?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, that was his dad. Well, you know, I've heard that that was a big way of socializing. They had people get together. They did. And that's the only recreation they had. That'd be when they could go to somebody's house, maybe dance all night long, you know. Have breakfast. Put the kids all down somewhere in the bedroom on the floor, you know. Oh, they had big times and they had a sociable time. Everybody, you know, communicated. And they'd take potluck and have their lunches, you know, have the body furnished. The whole family's went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would this be everybody in the whole neighborhood?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yes, whoever wanted to come. Whoever wanted to come. One person's place one time, another person's place another time. Just had good sociable times. Well, you know, they were all homesteaders here and there was no other recreation. And they sure made their own. And it was sure nice. Of course, that was a little before my time before we got up here. But I knew about it. Knew the people. Well, now, when you came, it was a little different. They weren't doing that the same way anymore. Well, they held it at the schoolhouse. We had the schoolhouse and we held our meetings up there and everything. And then they had this building that was supposed to be a butcher shop. They used that as a center to later on in later years. That's how long I imagine, like, say, 19, up to 1919. It was holding it the same way, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would it be like the same? Would the dances go one night at the schoolhouse, too?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, no, they would just play it over until about 11, 11 .30 or 12.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would that be more or less a regular kind of thing on a weekend? You know, say, just on the weekends? Or would it be a special occasion?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, it would be weekends when they decide to have a dance at a certain time. And they tell everybody, you know. And never charge for it. It's always free, you know.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And... Well, I and my dad built us a dance hall up here. We run that for 11 years. No, but that was in 1925. I heard of that place. And just the other day I was talking to Albin Queen. You know, Albin Queen there in Deary. And Mr. Lawrence from the Deary store. He was down there helping him put in a new water faucet on the kitchen sink. And Albin started talking about the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they call that place?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Camp Fire Plaza. We had a... It was round. It was 85 feet in diameter. And we had a fireplace right in the middle. It was open fire.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: People would dance when it got cold. They'd all flock up around that. And when the dance started, they'd all throw out of it. But you know, if we didn't have 150 couples, we didn't have no crowd in that place. And I talked to quite a few kids that went to that. And they said, you know, something might just live for that place from when I was a kid. But we run a clean place. We wouldn't have to get away from it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean? No booze here?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, no booze or anything like that. Well, they'd drink some, but then whenever it gets a little too much, well, that's it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You and your father built the place together? Did you build it yourselves?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, we had some men helping us, but we built it. And it was funny, we had... Well, the logs was about, oh, I'd say 12 feet long. And they laid around to put these rafters on, see. And you know, my dad was good enough when he'd come around there. I helped solve the wedging, you know, so they fit. And when we'd come around, we'd put the last one up and they went right to yours also. He was that good at that kind of work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about them maple floors? Weren't they maple?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I heard that floors were real beautiful. Well, they were hardly the outside.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And then we had the softwood in the middle. And then where we skated, you see, was hardwood. We skated too.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And a lot of that was curling maple.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I heard that, I guess, Alvin, Pauline said that you used some of the bands in Spokane to play there?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, yeah. And we had a guy that played three years from us, first from Lewiston. His name was Walt Allen. Walt Allen was a dance man out there. I know I was standing up there with him one night and he was a girl coming around. And of course, they didn't have no microphones then. They were just them big oranges. He'd hold them, put that guy on the high end. I said, you're getting pretty smart, you two. And we said, don't matter. She'd be right around here, backing right up this course. She can get pretty quick. And boy, she did. So I didn't do that anymore. I've heard that about them bands in the early days before. They're not so different than the bands today, you know. Well, he took all the dance crowd out of Lewiston. He run that for years. He got by with it, see. I was thinking about him. He had a young bunch of kids that he'd practice up, see. So he couldn't come the first night. So he said he'd send them kids. Boy, them kids went over a lot bigger than he ever did. Boy, them kids are good now. We tried to hire them. He said, I've got to get rid of them. He said, they're taken away from me. He said, they're all on ships now, going and playing in bands and ships.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, but he was good. He helped the crowd.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of music was it that he played?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, it was all the latest music. He always kept up with his music, you know.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And of course, this is later years, you see, in about 1926, wasn't it, that we built that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, these people, how far away did people come to go?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: To go to the school camp. And Lewiston, and all just all over. We put on the Fourth of July celebration up here on the fall. It was real hot. And we took every, it wasn't hard to anybody to let Lewiston, they all come up here.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: You mean in Genesee?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, in Genesee. We was down there to their fair. Yeah, we was down there one day, and I said, no, we better go home. And they was advertising the dance down there. And of course, them guys come up here a lot of them.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: So I seen them the next Saturday after, and I said, how's the dance going down there?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He said, I think we had three couples.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He said, well, this was a kind of out of way place, you know, and they like to get out of town. And they like to come out, you know, really drive quite a ways, you know, to a dance.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Is that what made it so successful, you think?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: It must have been more good than that, too.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: It must have been, it must have had a real good feeling in the place. Well, it did. It was a free feeling, but they knew everybody had to behave. You know, they, we run a good clean place, so we could have nice people or whoever wanted to come.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, run a place like that, you can't let, let, let them do as they please, because the good people will go somewhere else. You got your wife or your kids with you, you don't want somebody around to swear on you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would there be kids there, too?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, yeah. Little kids, I mean, they bring them family and somebody that they have.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yes, there was people from all over who used to come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what happened when the, when the depression come along? Did that hurt the business bad or what?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, you guys, you'd be surprised. Them young guys would count, count the pennies off. They didn't take it to count the money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the business got better?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, it didn't get better, but it's held right up. I guess I've heard that too, one time, they brought people on the same day, on having a good time, it means more.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: We sold them in 1937.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: But my dad was pretty old, and I didn't know we had to do the work. But it got off of the steel. I told them one day, one day, I was on the looking, on the lock on the bath house. I said, on the Fourth of July, I said, if we ever sell this, I'm going back so far in the woods, I'll never see a person all day. Yes, but we met lots of nice people. Lots of nice people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you able to make a living doing that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, you worked in the winter, you logged, and we went through the summer.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, we, I wanted to wait in there a lot of nights, a thousand dollars in the party.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: We, well, we earned it. We had a little farm, and of course, his father had a farm, and that was the main thing. But we just run this on, scaled on Tuesdays and Thursday nights, and danced on Saturday night. And then, of course, different organizations would come and rent the building there. Yeah. But they had their farms, and it all worked together. The Elks in Moscow would rent that.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And they'd come out here, and there was an old doctor here one night, he was out there, and the dance was over, and he went home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, what's the matter?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He said, I ain't going home, I'm going to stay right here. I'm going to stay right here. But, well, we had lots of nice people. There used to be a bunch of goat and elk, and they'd never miss. Every time we'd open, they'd be there. And I don't know, there must have been about 150 of them. And there was a nice bunch of people there, I would say. There were really, really good people. They never kicked on anything, everything was fine. And one of them would get... Enjoyed people like that, and enjoyed it, and they really did. One of them would get a little drunk, and you'd go for one of the others, and tell another, listen, you've got to do something with him. I got to take him out in the car and lock him in there, and make him stay there for a bit. No, we never allowed no rough stuff in the car, never. Some of them small, some small buildings and places I know did have fights. I mean, maybe outside, but they had a rough. You know, like around the north end of the county, so I don't know if you ever hear about it. You've probably heard a lot more about it than I have, but I've heard, you know, say, Honaway and Princeton, and those guys mix it up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A whole bunch would come from one place and go down and start looking for trouble, right?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, that's one thing that helped us. Moscow, a lot of Moscow universities, and they'd kind of, well, get off by themselves. Pretty soon there'd be such a big crowd, and if they didn't dance with a girl, they'd never find her anymore. There'd be too many in there. That didn't make, they had to make, so.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, we sure had lots of nice people, I'll say that. Yeah, it sounds like, it sounds like the biggest, you know, and the most sociable meeting place that I've heard about in the area. Well, I think it was, now they did have a hall at Troy. It seemed like it never went over like it should. I haven't heard too much about that. No, it seemed like they would rent, people would rent it, and first thing you know it was closed up, or they had, they had got new renders, or had sold it or something. Well, I'll tell you about it.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: There's a guy from down in Spokane, and he's telling this musician man, he said, I'll show them farmers how to run a dance hall, and he said, this old guy said, well now, don't be too sure about that, because he said, I think that park in Spokane is the only one that can head them out.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, yeah. Here's a Troy. Oh, yeah. They built one down there after we got this one out the back there, but it never did go over. Well, I suppose it did go over, but did not. We was down there the night before we opened, and you know, he had a pretty fair crowd, but it wasn't up next street. And the next night we opened, and all his crowd never stopped there, they come right on up there, see me. So the next morning, I went up to clean up, and I went up there and unlocked my door, and he said, boy, I'm glad you come on, we want to see what you got here. I said, well, you see a pretty dirty place. Well, he said, I don't care for that. So we went in, and he never gave another dance after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He did?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that a place now that where the Grange Hall is at, or is that someplace else?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, it stood up above, just as you go out, you know, under the trestle. It was on the left -hand side. I see. I don't know how long that stood there, how long it was. Well, then there was a man from Spokane who brought us out, and he stayed here a while, and he didn't go over. Of course, it was depression time, you know, and he went to Troy. He paid for it on our reputation.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: But that was a lot of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you decide to give it up?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was that, you did?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, it was just too hard to work for. It was just too hard to work. I wanted to get out of it, because I did the cooking, and that part of our dining room part I took care of. And we just got tired of 11 summers, you know, and raising a family, and so we just decided we...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were there, would it be just nags and nicks and oh, you served some?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: In the evening, we served cafeteria style, and they could have sandwiches, pie, or jello, or whatever they want. We had, you know, just a lunch all set out, and they'd come by and pick it up, whatever they wanted, and pay for it. You know, in a half hour, that crowd would be gone. Maybe five or six hundred people had their lunch and got there. Boy's dad wanted to serve regular meals, and that didn't go. I knew it wasn't, because when people go to dance, they go for a good time, and when they're through, they're ready to go home. And so he tried it, but it didn't go. Well, how many...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what would be a good crowd there? What would have been a good crowd?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, we figured around a thousand people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On a Saturday night?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, sometimes not that much, Dad. Well, we averaged right at the value. Two hundred and fifty people. We figured it could be, you know, we could figure on that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about for the roller skating?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, that was good. We really went over for that. Well, that was the only thing, that's the only roller skating place in the country. Nobody knows how to roller skate. And it was eighty -five feet, and it was round, see, and there wasn't no corners. They just kept on going. Yeah.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: We just went, moved down there, and some people come to us along the school and along the skating rink down there. I said, no, no, I heard them roller rolling out.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, we had sometimes three days, you know, celebration, like the Fourth of July or Labor Day. We'd have three days. And that went over. And of course, the Fourth of July, the Indian would come along races and things like that. It was kind of a recreation place, you know. But that, you know, will tell you when you do it for many years. Well, on the roller skating, people come from very far for that, too. Oh, yeah. It sounds great. I wish they had something like that now. Well, it really was nice. And now they have a small skating rink at Council, and I just wish they could have something like that. And run at as clean and nice a place as we did. But nowadays that would be hard, you know, under the conditions. We go down to the Club Troy every now and then, and that's, you know, once in a while, not often, but occasionally we do. And that's about the nicest place around, because the people of all ages there, and it's a real friendly place. People come from Moscow, people go there, you know. And that's a nice place you can enjoy yourself.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it's not, it still doesn't, you know, it's not like there's a lot of people, it's not really, it's not a community, really, right?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, you know, I and her, we was there all the time. Anytime we'd go to everybody, we'd meet and you'd know us. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yes, and living this close to Troy in Moscow, you know, we knew so many people.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And, oh, I enjoyed it, I really did. I enjoyed meeting the people, because I like people. And it was hard work, but they seemed to have so much fun and enjoy it, so it was enjoyable to me, you know, to be working. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember when the men were coming in and working on the railroad?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that, now there were a lot of people coming into the area then, wasn't there?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I mean, a lot of labor. Well, it was more Greeks.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They used a good many Greeks on the road, didn't they?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: On the railroad, yeah. Yeah, they had their foremen, white foremen, but they used quite a few Greeks.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And my mother, they had a camp down here at, how can I tell him where it's at? Do you know where McKinney's live? No, how far out of Deary is?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, just a little ways down here.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: You know where Haltzitz are?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, yes. Well, it's the next place. Now, there's where they had a camp there. And my mother used to bake bread for them, and we'd deliver it to them. And for the Greeks, you know, they had their own camps and did their own cooking, but they wanted to buy bread, so my mother baked bread for them. She baked about 40 loaves. Okay. And it was centered there. Okay, it's about half a mile from where I remember. I don't remember five cents a loaf I believe she got.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: When I was a kid, my dad had this bar, as he called it, and it was covered with tree branches, or shade. And he hired one of them off to play it for that band. And you know, he got so drunk, he had the man get on each side of him and take him up there. I never heard a man say that in my life.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Who are you? Don't you want any?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Sure.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: You want to see the door?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And this bowery, too, that was only up for just a summer. Yeah. But people would dance in that floor. They met there, just the old -timers, you know, at that time. I never heard of that. They called it a bowery at that time, you know. I don't know if they called it a pavilion now or something. But they'd all meet there and have a big time, celebrate, you know. It wasn't like it is now, the celebrations. You know, it was good and clean, just a lot of fun. And they'd have their games and things like that, too. There's so much graft in these celebrations now, they don't want them to operate.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, they're money -making.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you remember them coming in with the railroad and all that business?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, yeah. Do you think that... I was wondering about what people thought that was, you know, if they thought the country was going to really change, you know, now that the last was caught in. We nationally knew it would because it would put, well, it would get transportation into the country and out, see.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, and they could bring in the supplies and things that had to be brought in by team before, you know. Now the railroad gets much faster and it had more groceries and different kinds of things. The smaller stores just carried the staple stuff, you know. I know when my dad run the store, I used to go with him once in a while to get supplies.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He bought them in Troy. He'd go here down there and buy his groceries and come back in one day. Boy, it'd take a long while to make that trip. Huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He got the supplies for his store down there?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And then they had freighters, too, that brought stuff in. Yeah, that old Joe Wells, he used to freight all over this country. He had, I don't know, I guess I had heard he'd get down some of that. Yeah, he done that all. He had good horses and he was a good horseman. I'll never forget one time I was a kid, he used to stay at my folks' quite a lot when he was freightin'. I don't know why he'd pass his own place up there. He'd come up there and put his, my folks run the kind of boardin' place, too, where they'd stop.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And he was hookin' up his horse in the morning, it was real cold in the fall, and he picked up the neck yoke. He put the neck yoke up on one horse and he picked up the neck hook on the other horse and grabbed ahold of that strap that the neck yoke goes into and the old horse hauled off and bit him. He took that neck yoke and he hit that horse and he knocked it down. Well, he didn't temper alright, the horse had been lame. Yeah, I've heard that seven of these horses were as good as potlatch's horses. Yeah. Well, that potlatch used to hit them all for nice horses. I know I, when I got big enough, well I started workin' in the woods when I was just a small kid, I couldn't even pick up the riggin'. But after I growed up, well I used to drive a horse for potlatch. And I'd say, the old barn boss, I'd like to drive that thing, it wouldn't be only about a day or two till I'd have it. And finally, my own death, a lot of men can't drive horses, you'd be surprised, there's a lot of men that can't get nothin' out of a horse. And they'd kinda ruin them and they'd give me a team and, you know, spoil, give them to me, you see.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And I got kinda tired of that and I said to the boss one day, how come I get all these horses if somebody spoils?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, he said, you don't worry now, you're gettin' as much fun as any man in camp doin' that. And I was too. But boy, they had some pretty horses out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean he'd watch you take a team that was in bad shape?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, they weren't in bad shape, they were just spoiled. A lot of men don't use no judges with a horse. They'll hook them on to somethin' they can't pull and then knock on them because they don't. And I said, well, a horse is a lot smarter than a lot of people think they are. I know a superintendent said to me one time, when I went to work, I said, now I want off to go elk hunting. My dad liked to hunt, so I go every fall with him. And so I went on the way and on this hunt, I come back and he stopped and said, boy, you better get over there and take that team here.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And I said, why?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And they said, that old guy, he ain't gettin' nothin' on him. They said, they won't do nothin' for him. So the next day I went over there and this guy had this team hooked on to four little top dogs. I could have pin it full to myself. And he went down with them. So the next time he'd come up, I said, well, I'll take him down this time. And the swamp reed kept near on the mornin'. I think that's about all them paul floyd and all and they don't know no one too long. So I took him into the land and came back. He said, this old guy is satin' there. He said, my god, them horses even look different when you drive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was the superintendent?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Nago. I've heard that he was quite a man for horses. He was a good horseman. The air was gettin' better back in the mornin' already. Yeah, I bet he was. I know one time he gave me a, well he didn't, the barn boss gave me a span of black poles. He was a nice looking team, but the guy that drove him the year before, he didn't get anything on them. He spoiled them. So I took him out and when you pulled him real hard, he'd throw his head right over the other horse and down they'd go and get tangled up. And I said to my swamper, who was standin' there, we was killin' down lorries. And I said, you get over on the other side and when he sticks his head over there, you haul off and hit him with your can hook and knock him back. Boy he didn't, he knocked him colder or worse, I thought he killed him. But he never did do that again. And the superintendent was down there and I was pullin' along past the land and pullin' him up with a little grave.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, Floyd, what are you pullin' them horses up this grave that these lorries heard?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I said, I'm learnin' them to pull out here on level ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, Doc, that's colder than a what?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Wedge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wedge?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I thought he said it.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I never heard that expression before.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: You know, I was talkin' to one old lumberjack in Lewiston who used to work, he worked up here and he worked over on Clearwater too. You know, up the river, he was on the river, dry. Anyway, I'd heard people sayin' that Potlatch's horses were real outstanding horses. He said that they were a lot better up here than they were down there. Oh they were, yeah. They didn't have the teamsters down there up here. But one thing about it, if they found out you could drive team, that's all they'd give you. They'd give you good money, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Say, uh, why don't you sit down and talk to these folks for a while?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I don't mean to monopolize. Oh, well that's fine. I'll come and see them again. Yeah, we'll be, we'll see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When are you comin' down?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I don't remember. You had a war there.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
