words: Well, I was workin '... You was workin' before we was married a long time. Not for Potlatch. Well, your dad was gettin' his job... Oh, well I worked for Potlatch, I was part of the whole of my life.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And it went round about way, but I didn't stay in camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know when you started working? You didn't work and stay in camps?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, when I worked for the company I did, yeah. I worked for the team, but my dad, he used to have a log and I'll set it his own. We done that.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They work together.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: What I heard about Potlatch camps, you're probably better off stayin' out of them as far as living conditions if you could... Well, the only thing is I raised my family. My family grew up and I had to run from home. That's the only thing I didn't like about it. And them old guys just didn't do nothin' else. They was right at home in them camps, and I never was.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yes, but we was awful glad to have the job, you know, because Potlatch sure helped a lot of the people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you remember the IWWs?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Were they there when you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What'd you think of them?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, they done a lot of good. I'll tell you somethin', them companies are all right, but they're lookin' after their own interests. They don't care about workin'. That was it. I've heard almost nothing but good about the IWWs. Well, they got conditions a lot better. Now, when I was real small, they had a camp over there called the Camp 6, and they had a big bunkhouse and there was 50 or 60 men in one house. You go and they'd hang up them wet clothes and them old socks and stuff in there and you couldn't hardly get your breath when you'd walk in. And that's the way the company wanted it, but then that wasn't the way to live.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yes, but they changed that later on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had their bunkhouses, didn't they?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They had bunkhouses, more bunkhouses. They never got the small bunkhouses till World War II. They never got smaller bunkhouses until World War II.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the strike they had in 17?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I was here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you walk off the job too?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I wasn't working for them men. I was working for Company Logan, but then I wasn't. They didn't bother us. We weren't big enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did all the guys do when they quit work? Did they stick around in the country?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, no. Them men, at times, they didn't stay in any one place too long. But at that time, they all got called back. The railroaders were the only ones that didn't get called back. Some of the railroaders didn't get called back. But as far as the loggers were, I think all them, in fact, they all got called back. That's what they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they do?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They met. Well, I don't know. They kind of got in for them and there were a lot of things that happened that got laid on to them that they didn't do. I know that. I've heard a lot of people say that.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Did the fire or anything?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, the loggers didn't matter. I remember there was quite a bit of smut in the fields at that time and there was fire starting in the fields because of the smut. Well, there could have been. But they blamed it on the other people. Anything that happened. Well, I heard about the bullpen in Moscow and they went through some of the irony and the insanity. Yeah, I remember that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the Bowdills? The Bowdills? You and Charlene Bowdills?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, we knew all of them. I didn't know them personally.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't stay too long, did they, after?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I can't remember. They were gone by the teens, anyway.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: It was here a few years after we moved up here.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: There was a walker here by the name of T .P. Jones. He was a quiet man in the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember him being like?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I heard something about him. I heard he was a... Well, he was a pretty good man for the country, I think. But I'll have to tell you what happened one time. My dad was riding with him and he drove a buggy then. He was in this buggy and he stopped. He said, I won't stop here, love this. Well, then we stopped and he tied up the horses and we went out across the country there. And I went out there and there was an old guy asking if we cut some wood, some dry trees there. And he was out there and that guy, he never cut no dry trees. He cut the green ones and had them put up wood. Now he said, that's what a man gets for being good to somebody.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah. But I don't know. That day, when he left here, he went to Montaigne. He went in the oil business. He never did come back after that. Well, I heard he got in all kinds of trouble when he was in there. He said, oh well, business there. Yeah, people got in for him pretty bad. They still remember in Bovill, and there were several people who remember that, how when he was an old man, you know, Mrs. Jones doled out nickels and dimes to him. He wanted anything you could ask for money for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to school writing in Helmer? Was that where the school was?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: When I first moved here in the spring, I went to the log schoolhouse up here at what was the old flag place. Now it's Hamilton Place, up near Cornell. There's an old log schoolhouse there and he went there too. And then next year, they built this one down here. I was thinking we went that next fall up there.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I don't know.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: We started the first school year down here, didn't we?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I don't know where you did. I started up there.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I know, but I mean, when the school started here, wasn't it the first year, school year?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They built this in 19, in the 1980s?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah. 19th on the building. And Floyd's first term, he went to this old log schoolhouse down here at Deary. But Joe Wells' place.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, I went two terms there.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: When you started to go to school, you know, I heard people talk about the one room schoolhouse and I heard it called good and bad too. You know, that it was hard to learn or that kids could learn a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think of it when you were going there? Was there too many kids to learn very much or did you have a chance to learn a lot?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, I think we learned real well. I really, I got my education in that kind of school. And I really think we got more in them days, real good teaching, than they do now. I might be old fashioned, I don't know. But we got it. We had to have it. The teachers seemed to have that.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Did you have a chance to, they had all the grades just in the one room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you learn a lot from hearing the other grades recite?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I think we did. I really do. That is if you wanted to learn, if there's some child that didn't care why they didn't get it anyway. I really think that you do. You listen to the lower grades too when you're in a room like that with all the grades. And then of course it gets, then you just get up to your level and that's it. I really think that we really got a lot more out of school then. There's too much play now, I think. That's just my old fashioned idea.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I don't think there's as much attention to the basic reading and writing.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, the schools are a lot more advanced now and more advanced.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, they get more learning of different things now than we did. We just got the facts, what we had to have.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: But then the teachers like Anna Maria Hobson or Annemarie Anderson, they were so young. Nowadays teachers are quite a bit older it seems.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of your teachers young? Were you young people? I mean 20 years old?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I didn't think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, weren't most of the teachers then young, really young people like Anna Maria?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yes, most of them I think they were. Oh, they were possibly, now I know I went to school to the Cole girls in Troy and it was, oh I imagine they went up to, the girls was up, there was three of them. And the youngest was constant and she was just 18. And then there was the other two girls was, I would say, 35 probably.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh really, that old?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence

Sixty, that's what he was just saying, sixty men in the bunkhouse. Yeah, you bet. And they still had it in 1925.

speaker: Sam Schrager

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence

I know my uncle plunked it, Jim Lee, he plunked it over here at six. And, of course, he'd come over to our house, you know, to visit. And one day we was, my mother was cutting out a pattern, you know, for a dress. And she looked and she saw a bug crawl. And she looked and it was a body lice. And he had got them at camp. And, boy, now, there wasn't cleaning house going on there. But they just, I don't know, there was just so much of that stuff at that time. Well, it was unsanitary conditions, you know. Because they didn't have bathtubs and they didn't, and they carried their own bedding.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And half the time they didn't get their clothes washed. Men from camp to camp, you know.

words: How big an outfit did you have?

words: Well, we had about three teams, three or four, and we had our sawyers. The biggest job we put in was up at Helker. The biggest job ever put in,

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: it was Ralph Holland's money that bought the timber, that is, it paid for it, and there was a guy along that didn't know very much about it.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: We finished up two or three jobs, and then they moved camp, and they had a big flat down there. It had a lot of big white pine on it, and they went in there and tried to log it the year before, but they couldn't handle that big timber. And so we cut our dray. We used drays to haul them, I think, and that was runners. We cut them trees and hewed them out up the upper camp, and that train crew went in to pick them up, and they handled them just like they was handling eggs. They just wouldn't drop them or do nothing with them.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: We had a log, one yellow white pine in there that, do you remember how much that big log was killed?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: It's in here. Just past it somewhere. I was trying to find Helmer here to see what time Miller's came in here.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: But when they put that log in, all the big guys at the camp came out.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: You've got 1 ,800 board feet.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Is that the one?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, that's the one here.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Is that the one you mean?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, that's the one down here. We started to see all these guys. Yeah, at the camp. They'd all come down and watch us put that in. They didn't know how to fix their chains on their dray, and they'd put them on, and the logs would roll and roll, upset the dray, and they just didn't have no luck at all. But the way to do it, you have your hole about this far in your bunk, and you put this chain over this way and this one over this way, see, and you had them. I rolled that big log on my dray, and they was coming up the road when I was coming out, and the team and I was just open the log, and they couldn't hardly get over it. I just had one log run through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did your family learn how to log good? Where did they get to be good loggers in their family? Did your father know how?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, yeah, and then the experience we had growing up, we knew how to take advantage of most everything. I know, I'll tell you now how.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: There was a guy working in camp, and he put on big loads, and I said to him, what do you put on some big loads for?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, he said it looks good when I pull out on the manual with that big load on. So about the third load after that, why, he put on all them big loads and started down, and pretty soon after he'd come, he said, I guess what, I've got to have a pull. I said, I told you you shouldn't put on such big loads. And I had a brand new decking line, that's a steel test chain, and I put a hitch on that of six to one, and I pulled that log up against that guy's, pulled that tray up against that horse's hind ends and hit him, and pretty soon he fell down off of that load and he just knocked the sap out of him.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: So he finally, I pulled it again and he took it along, and you know, I never did pick up that chain, it just stretched it just like a rod.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, I know. I just had to cut that. I don't wonder how a guy like that could get by, you know, I mean. Well, he was an awful good hook man, that was one thing about him. He'd play with the log going all the way up the skids, but he liked to show off. Yeah, he was quite a ginger. And he said to me, after I, when he come back up there, after he went in, he said, by God, Ford, I didn't think that little thing could pull that much. They hadn't been drove very much, they hadn't been in the woods. He said, that thing sure surprised me. Yeah, but Ford had his old block on it too. Yeah, I had it fixed up so that... Yeah, he had his old block on it. Well, from what you're saying, I get the idea that even without a very big outfit, you could pull, you could take care of pretty much any size timber. Oh yeah, you could. If you'd done it right. Yeah, I know I was up at the camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that camp up there on the meadows where you saw it?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Camp 2. Well, anyway, they got hired a guy from... Camp 2, Camp 10. Yeah, and he come up there and we had to give him some of our skidways. We had them all cleaned up, we was about to clean up. So we said, yeah, we'll give you a couple of them. So he pulled in and pulled down, took it down the skidway, and he was right up against us, snagged.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Them horses, they couldn't pull on, and I said, why don't you change your tongs?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, they said, you fix them. And I did, and I put a roll on it, rolled it right over. Now, he said, that's the difference between a man knowing how and what to do. Those hippies were their dogs. I wonder where they come from. I bet they were in North Texas. Well, I worked in North Texas wherever an ordinary man who made wages actually made good money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in your equipment, you didn't use any steam donkey and that kind of stuff?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, no, I never worked around there.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They were all right, but they were awful hard on timber. They knocked it down and pushed it over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the trick to making good money, like what you're saying, if you could make money, fair money or good money, what was the trick to making good money? What did you have to do to come out way ahead? Did you have to be able to do it fast? Was that the main deal?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, it didn't. The main thing was, now, there was a lot of men that couldn't drive horses because they didn't use no judgement with them. They'd put on more and they'd put on a hook on to something and wouldn't get it. They'd put in a lot of time messing around without getting anywhere, see.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I know I was working for a gipple down here. It's Arthur Burke's place.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: There was a crew, a loading crew, and they said to me, if you bring your team up to skid that butt out, I don't think we can haul it. It's a big tan rag butt. And so I put the tongs on it and rolled it on the skid and went right into the band. That guy running down there, he said, that's the first time I've ever seen them horse this pull like that. It's just things as that. That all helps. But I mean, another guy might have taken the same team and couldn't have done what you'd done with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the difference?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, putting it on a skid, it raises up off the ground. When I pulled around and turned the skid, the skid rolled right along with it, see. I see, yeah. Yeah. But them horses, you can't fool them too long. They know that pretty quick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think there was a lot of difference when you were working between working a gipple or working for the company directly?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I mean, did you prefer one of the... Well, you made a lot more money gippling.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Your work charge, too. With the company, they didn't pay enough. They didn't figure on you making too much money. I know I was working for Axel over there on Tree Bear one summer. And they gave us two and a half for his trip. And the swamper mine, that year, they put all the teamsters in one buck house and the swampers in another. And this swamper mine, he stayed in the swamper's buck house. He said, there ain't no guys for it. He said, we're making less money than anybody can. And, oh, I said, I don't think so, Bill. I said to him, there was a struck a spring up this draw over his end. He turned the water in and he put in some, for the dig, he put in some skids to raise them up, see. And I had like a tape, all ten or five or six of them big logs that were scaled four or five hundred feet a piece. And just walk right down there, you see. So when he got his check, he said to me, I come out in the car and he'd come out on the speeder. I seen him the next day and he'd come over that night. He said, bye, guys, for it, I've been here. Pulled the hinges right off of that office tonight. He said, when I looked at that check, I put in your fellow. And the old boss the next year, the next morning, he said to me, he said, bye, Jesus, you said you made more money than I made less money. Well, I said, I worked for it, too. But, you know, that old guy was after me from then on. He never did forget that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did he do? Give you less money for doing the same job?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, they'd give me two and a half, which was pretty good money, see, at that time. And I got more money than... But, I mean, you said after that. Well, after that, the other jobs, he'd give me jobs and offer me the money that I know I could make. And he knew it, too. He finally put me down on a strip. There was a guy, a couple of guys that had been on there for two weeks and they never put in a log. And, of course, they was drawing wages all the time, see. And mud, I never got to mud. Them horses, you couldn't tell what color they was when I came up with them. It was a piece of muddy. And I stayed and they got about half done. I'd come home and I quit. But he wasn't going to give me enough. I said, I'll go for wages, but I ain't going down there and take that job the way it is. They took all the face off of them all for the year before and it was way back, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then this guy that was down there, the teamster, the boss came up there and said, what's the matter? I said, can't you see what's the matter?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I was going with about 25 logs dug together then, see, and I couldn't stop any place. So I went down to where it was steeper and stopped.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And he said, what's the matter? I said, can't you see what's the matter?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And next morning, they sent that guy down to the other camp. That was an axle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That wasn't Axel, was it?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: That was Axel. Well, Axel was the boss. He's not the one they sent down to the other camp. No, it was Steve Plisko. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was Axel the one that was giving you a hard time?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah. He's trying to get even with me, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you think about Ed Halsett? What do you think about Ed Halsett knowing a lot about the old timers?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I've talked to him. Oh, you've already talked to Ed. You know, I got his papers for the Historical Society in Moscow, but he didn't fill them all out. And so I never did get them in. That was before I left here. I wondered if you'd talked to him. Yes, I have. Well, he should know a lot of us, same as Arthur Burke. He knows a fair bit, yeah. Arthur turned in an awful good report, I thought. Yeah. Yes, sir, he really did. About the railroad and everything, that's pretty well correct. He's awful good about the days. This little story in here that I told about Freddie Grannis, he got that little mixed up. I felt bad about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was the story in it? Pardon? What's the way you told it?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, I told it that he threw the knife, and he did, and hit the back of the seat and just scared me nearly to death. But it was a clothes knife, see, and I told him that. And some way, I don't know how he got that, but I'll have to find it over here. I felt bad about being ripped that way because I like to have things straight and he may let them...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would you tell me that story from the beginning?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I don't know that I remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the story?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Don't read it to me. Okay, well, he was just a kid, we played together all night, Freddie and I. And he went, and this Mr. Aldrin taught, he was a very cross man, you know. And he was really hard on the youngsters, and so he had taught out there on the ridge from Troy, on one of the ridges. And so we had heard that he had threw a knife at one of the kids. But we just laughed it off, you know, so I was telling Freddie about it, and he said, well, I'll just tell you something. He said if you throw the knife at me, I'll pick it up and throw it back. And so it wasn't too long until Freddie was playing flyaway jack and flyaway jill, you know, putting papers on your fingers. And then he's showing one of the children across, one of his friends across, and he'd say, flyaway...

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Anyway, I watched him pretty soon. He went on just looking at his book, and he reached in his pockets, you know. And he pulled his knife out, and bang, it hit that seat. And I'm telling you, and then of course he'd come back and he hit Freddie with his book, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he had his finger between the books like this, and when he hit Freddie, why, I know it hurt, but so then I said to Freddie, well, what about it?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And I said, you said you were going to throw that back, and he just said, well, he said it didn't make a difference. He hurt his fingers, but it hurt my head, you know. But it sure scared him. But he said an open knife, but it wasn't, it was a closed jack knife, you know. And I felt kind of bad about that, but... And at the same time, Freddie's read this book, you know, and he's as old as I am, you know. And I just wondered what he thought about that, because that wasn't exactly right, but... You know, how kids will do.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, I imagine that, well, I've heard anyway that some of the women teachers really had a hard time keeping the schoolhouse, you know, in order.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, they did, because the children were so much older that went to school then, than they are, you know, in the elementary school. Some of them would be 17 years old, you know, and they hadn't got through the eighth grade yet, or through the school, or at first it wasn't graded, you know, really.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: But... You know, talking about the stories in this book, there was that story about the... That they think that some bohunks got, at least one of them got killed, and that rumor. Did you ever hear that when you were... Oh, yes. Yes,

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: we heard that. We don't know how true it was, but it was at that time they said that they were buried under the fill over here.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I'll tell you what happened to me one time. My dad ran that store and he had an order of groceries to take up, and I was just taking them up in the wagon. And there was one of them bosses come along and he stopped and he said, you better stop, there's a charge of powder up there. I was sitting there looking around and I said, hey, there goes a couple of guys down there right where it's at. And I don't know what had done trouble, but it went off when they was right about opposite. He never moved.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see the look on his face? Did he look scared?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, well, he was back out there talking to me, see. I thought I might have killed him, but I don't know where it did or not. Oh, you saw there were a couple of guys down there. Oh, yeah, I saw them. And I said to him, look at that, there goes a couple of guys down there. He just looked at them and didn't say anything about that time that it went off.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yes, but you don't know it was those. You didn't think it was them.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence
words: Who?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Those two that were buried over there.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I don't think so. I don't know if I even heard them or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was the story supposed to have been that they had an argument?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: That's what John Miller records. No, that's about all we knew too, just about what Arthur told you. Well, these people, like the Greeks, they were Italians too, I guess, that worked on the railroad. Did they mix with the other people or did they, because they didn't speak English very much. They didn't speak pretty much to themselves.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: In a place they would go to the saloon. They would go to the saloon, that is, the bunch of them would go.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: My dad ran a saloon and them guys would come down there and get drunk, and of course there wasn't no kids in the country. And they'd see us and they'd take right out after us. And boy, we'd just run for home the farther we'd go, we'd trade it full on us. They got me out of the drinking, I never did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did these guys run after kids?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, it was just playing because they were drinking. There wasn't more kids in here, see, and they'd just be surprised to see kids and they'd take right out after us and talk to us. Did your father, I'd heard that there was real rough times when the railroads were being built in here, very real early, you know, when the first logging was done in here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father ever have a hard time there, running the saloon, the drunk guys?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, I don't think he ever did too much. Of course, I was pretty young then, I don't know much about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the saloon, what was the saloon look like? Was it just inside the store, part of the saloon?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, it was a building by itself.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: It was a log building without logs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have a bar?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, no, no, no. It sounds like your father was pretty, was an enterprising fellow with the stores. He was too much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there anything else at Janesville besides his store? I mean, was that the town or was there other stores too?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, he just started that on his homestead.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They had their house built and he built the store on, just a little store for the post office.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wondered, do you think he thought back then that there might be a real town there?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, I don't think so. He just did them for the business he had then, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he pick that name for the town, I wonder?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I mean, it was always called Janesville.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they do in those days? Did they pick the towns for, was it named after somebody?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, it could be anything. The way I understand it was they turned in a name and the person that was going to have the post office or the town would and if there wasn't another town in the state already had that name then they could get that name. Well, I guess that's what it was. I just wondered if there was somebody named Jan or Jane or something like that around. I don't know. I never knew about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they spell that J -A -N -S or J -A -N -E -S?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: J -A -N -E -S, V -I -L -L -E. See, I've seen it spelled both ways.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Now, there's a man who came here to talk to Carl and Mae about somebody here in early days had invented some barbed wire and he was hunting all kinds of barbed wire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Way back and all kinds and he said that he lived here, what was his name? Dent?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No. Dent. And that he had invented some barbed wire. He wanted to know if we knew anything about it. I have never heard about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No. And so I did get a book. He had the book here. This fellow did have it. He had a book with him. Yeah.

speaker: Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Was he sure it was Janesville, Idaho?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No. He was from Spokane.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence
words: Oh, what did he say his name was?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Hmm.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I've got it at home. I kept his letter. The man was supposed to patent the barbed wire. But it was never put up for sale. No. He just got the patent on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And his address was Janesville?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, he was supposed to be, the post office was Janesville. Janesville. And I got a book. I paid the librarian there to get me a book. And it did show this barbed wire, alright, and it was from Janesville. But I can't remember whether it was Idaho or not. But it was a wide wire, a peculiar wire with little barbs on it. He had a book with him, you know. He had that the same book then, I suppose, that I had got. And, oh, well his name was a common name. Yeah, I know too, and I can't think what it was. Well, I've seen Jim, Jim, Jim, what was Jim, Jim was his first name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Jim Harris. Harris, right. That's right. His name was Harris. The guy who was looking for the... He was collecting wires, see, and he had so much wire from different places, different kinds. And he wanted to see if he could get a piece of that. He wanted to get a piece of that. But in our day we never knew anything about it. Yeah, we bet. Yeah, Jim Harris was, I suppose, the name of Houston's folk band.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, I was going to ask you about your parents' place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he keep the livery stable for very long here in Helmer?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He had it for about... Oh, he had it for about, well, until, well, let's see.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I don't think he had it over a couple of years. Oh, yes, he did.

speaker: Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Did he have it that long?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he open a grocery himself here, or did he, what did he do after the livery?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, he had a little grocery store right in front of the house. He had built on a little store. He sat right up here on the corner. Right here in this corner of this place. Across the road there. See, the barn was right out there. Right on the corner of the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he competing with Miller?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No. Fudge Dad had a store up here then on the corner. He had bought that. And he had a store. No, they just had, well, they just had the two little stores.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, your father had the store here too, huh?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah. He sure was in a lot of businesses. I can't get over it. Yeah, he was in a lot of business.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: He must have really seen a lot too, seen this country grow from nothing.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And I think he had the first car that ever come to the country too. Yep.

speaker: Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: What was the name of that? Studebaker?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Overland.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you say, it wasn't 1911 Overland?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: A nineteen -nine, I think. A nineteen -nine Overland.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could he drive it around here then?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Was the roads good enough to...

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: There's a picture of one of these cars there and I see it's in that book isn't it?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Where they had them in Deary. Of course, that was later than mine.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, I could just talk of the past if I knew anything for all night, I think. Yeah. But I don't know too much about it.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, you know quite a lot about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father wind up making much money from the ventures?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Or did he... The last one was what they call the Willis -Knight. Yeah, then he got the Model D board. Yeah. Yeah, I remember thinking he was Deary of that Willis -Knight. Did... Was there other cars in here right afterwards or did he have the other car for...

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I've heard when the first cars come in a lot of places a lot of people didn't believe in them. You know, well... You thought you were crazy you had a car. But he didn't know very much about it. I'll tell you what happened. This ain't very nice. But he... when you got a car then you got a tire pump, see. Oh, now don't you tell that. Don't you get... tell that. Oh, yeah. Don't you tell that. That's dirty. So he brought it home and he had a flat tire. Well, before he come home, I and my brother was playing around there and we had that pump. So we decided we'd go out there's an old pig tent out there and we figured we'd go out and blow that old sow up. So we went out there and... There's the old cars. I suppose it was burners. You got up and left. And Dad come home and he had a flat tire. And so he put it on. He didn't think about putting it past the tubes. He put the tire on... he put the pump on the tire and he pumped and pumped. He said, I don't believe that son of a bitch is working. And he took it off and put it in his mouth. And I looked at him and I thought, boy, if you didn't know where that fence you were putting it from. Now in that nice store for you too. It's funny.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, that's an old picture of Gary. 1914.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah, in them days.

speaker: Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Now, wasn't it Miller's?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Somebody comes from Moscow that had a car and they charge so much to ride in it at the fair down there. They held a little fair in Gary. I think so. That's when the cars... Oh, there was no cars, you know, but...

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I've heard people would take a team wagon for miles to see a car and when they come in.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: The first time I ever drove in the car, I drove it in 1920 with a 490 Chevrolet.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, our first was in 1923.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Our first car was a 1923. First time I had enough money we could buy one.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I bought a huge. Well, Henry Jones and I bought it together. We bought a huge 1918 Model 490 Chevrolet and I was working on the section on the Yale Hill. And...

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Neil and Parker put on a black-face minstrel ... for a donation of something I own a lot. And so... And I come up here. And I thought that was the damn road that I ever drove over. It was a surely long road there. I was driving a 4 -night Chevrolet. I was driving a 4-night Chevrolet. Yeah. That's the first time I drove with children. Yeah. And then I drove on off and on after that. Yeah. 1920. And I heard a lot of people had a hard time learning how to drive at first. The car I really liked that I had any trouble riding. And... Bennett.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: We got it from Lloyd and Lee Bennett there in Potts.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: They just took us out. So it wasn't moving, we just took off.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: One more thing I thought of was Helmer. Bill Helmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he live in this town?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, they just named it after him. Yeah. You see... Well, he was a human physicist. And... And Phil Peterson, he used to stay at our place too, you know. You know where my old... I told you, I was down there on the Frank Burchville, I'm down there on the Burchville place. That's where I was raised.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And Phil Peterson, he rode a bicycle. He was a timberman. And he stayed at our place. And Bill Helmer stayed at our place.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And I suppose Helmer, he probably stayed at Moore's too, I suppose. No, he didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah. Yeah, he stayed at Moore's. Yeah, him and that red -headed... Well, Phil Peterson was red -headed. No, this was... Oh, you know where he was, in Malory. Oh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which one? Oh, you mean Frank?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I don't remember now, but I remember he was red -headed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they stay for like during the winter or something?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No, they'd come in and... They'd come in and... Well, they'd stay a week or two at a time. They'd stay two weeks. Then they'd be gone somewhere else. Then they'd be gone somewhere else.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Because around our place it was all timber. There'd only been one camp in there before that.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And that was Camp One. They called it old Camp One down there. I heard that Helmer was really pretty good in the woods. Oh, yeah, he was. He was. I'll tell you what they say. I never dealt with him, but they say he was an awful walker. Yeah, he was. And you know, if he'd find a section post, he'd fix it so by God if he was looking for it, and run the line, you couldn't even find it when you come to it. I know I've run a lot of his lines.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Bill Helmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why would he do that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, I suppose to make it miserable for somebody else that's trying to do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would you cover it up?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Well, he'd blaze along. When he got to the quarter post, he just quit that. I've heard that he could find his own marks any place. I mean, 20, 30 years later. Well, I suppose he knew about where it was at after the town line. I'll tell you. But he was pretty accurate on it. Yeah, he was.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: If I was just a kid one day.

speaker: Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Dad, don't you think that's a mistake where he put this GR Russ Lawrence and his wife sold that property up there at McGarry Meda?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: That was your granddad. That should have been Warren Lawrence instead of Russ Lawrence.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when Helmer was, was Helmer ever much of a town as far as how many stores or what kind of activity went on right in town here? No, I don't think so, do you?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: I wouldn't... Well, I don't know. Of course, like I say, when I came up here in 24, the two stores were here. Yeah, there's more people than there is now. I don't see that. I don't see that many people here now. Yeah, I do too. I do too. You dang right. You bet.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: But, I suppose in earlier days,

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: they could make it quite a little bit because people, there was no competition. Boy, Duncan had moved out of here.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: See, he had a, didn't he have a pool hall and a barber shop?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yeah.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Boy, in Helmer here, there was a pool hall and a barber shop. Yeah, you see, he went to... Jim Parks. Jim Parks. He went to... Well, that's what I say, there was an hotel here at one time. And like I say, somebody said that there was a butcher shop set right straight across, right straight here over there on that other street. Of course, that was way before my time. But like I say... Well, see, what I'm thinking about is, you take some of these towns like Nora or Anderson, they died, disappeared, they're gone. But Helmer is still here. Helmer will be here, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, why did Helmer, I'm wondering why Helmer stuck it out when these other towns didn't? Well, now down there at Nora, there was no lots, there was no township, was there?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: No town laid out. Well, this was surveyed out in streets and avenues. And I think that's a... And then at that time, I think they done a pretty good business because the camps were here and... Oh, yeah.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: You see, when I come up here in 24 and 25, there was five camps up here working out of here in Crow Creek and... Besides the headquarter camp here, camp six. There's five camps working up here. There was one at camp ten and then there was camp five on wet meadows and there was camp four on bass and meadows.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: And there was camp three up there on well, barracks and meadows and camp seven on chain meadows.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: You bet. So quite a market for...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that mostly the lumberjacks that used the stores?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Yes, of course. At that time, there was a lot of married men in the woods, but they had to stay in camps.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, that one's a good one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was the cellar? Did you need the light turned on?

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: No, it don't seem to be too bad in there.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Are you on? No? You want to put it in this in vellum?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Wilkins Lawrence
words: Oh, sure. I could do that. Look, they wrote this. Let's see. It said, Mom came to Hillsboro, North Dakota.
